extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete in public festivals
verbatim:
Soc. Do you mean to say that the Epidaurians honor the god with a contest of rhapsodes also?
               Ion. Certainly, and of music Music with the Greeks included poetry. in general.
               Soc. Why then, you were competing in some contest, were you? And how went your competition?
               Ion. We carried off the first prize, Socrates.
            
 
               Soc. Well done: so now, mind that we win too at the Panathenaea. The Athenian festival of the Great Panathenaea was held every fourth year, and the Small Panathenaea probably every year, about July.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that rhapsodes as a professional group have the right and social function of competing for prizes (material and immaterial resources) at major Greek religious festivals like those in Epidaurus and Athens.

verbatim:
For the rhapsode ought to make himself an interpreter of the poet’s thought to his audience; and to do this properly without knowing what the poet means is impossible. So one cannot but envy all this.
               Ion. What you say is true, Socrates: I at any rate have found this the most laborious part of my art; and I consider I speak about Homer better than anybody
extracted_rules.reasoning: Socrates and Ion agree that the role of the rhapsode traditionally includes the 'right' or function of interpreting the meaning of the poets to the audience, a form of intellectual influence/resource.

verbatim:
Soc. Well then, would you, or one of the good seers, expound better what these two poets say, not only alike but differently, about the seer’s art?
               Ion. One of the seers.
               Soc. And if you were a seer, would you not, with an ability to expound what they say in agreement, know also how to expound the points on which they differ?
               Ion. Of course.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The dialogue establishes a social/logical rule in Greek society where those belonging to a specific professional group (seers, doctors, etc.) are the only ones with the authority ('resource') to judge or expound upon their own craft as depicted in literature.

verbatim:
Soc. And again, when several are talking about what kinds of foods are wholesome, and one of them speaks better than the rest, will it be for two different persons to distinguish the superiority of the best speaker and the inferiority of a worse one, or for the same?
               Ion. Obviously, I should say, for the same.
               Soc. Who is he? What is his name?
               Ion. A doctor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies doctors as the specific group possessing the authoritative capacity to judge discourse regarding wholesale/unwholesale food, a specific form of expert influence.

verbatim: Yes, very fully aware: for I look down upon them from the platform and see them at such moments crying and turning awestruck eyes upon me and yielding to the amazement of my tale. For I have to pay the closest attention to them; since, if I set them crying, I shall laugh myself because of the money I take, but if they laugh, I myself shall cry because of the money I lose.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Ion describes the economic reality of his profession, where his personal income is directly tied to the emotional reaction of his audience during a performance. This establishes that rhapsodes had the right to earn and retain money from public recitations.

verbatim: And to every art has been apportioned by God a power of knowing a particular business? For I take it that what we know by the art of piloting we cannot also know by that of medicine. . . Since, I suppose, if it were a knowledge of the same things—how could we say that one was different from another, when both could give us the same knowledge? . . . Then he who has not a particular art will be incapable of knowing aright the words or works of that art?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Socrates establishes a normative rule of Greek intellectual and professional life: that specific knowledge is the domain of specific practitioners (doctors, pilots, charioteers). Those within the group (holders of the 'art') have the authority to judge works related to that art, while those outside it do not.

verbatim: Now, Ion, will a doctor or a charioteer be the better judge whether Homer speaks correctly or not in these lines? Ion. A charioteer, of course. Soc. Because he has this art, or for some other reason? Ion. No, because it is his art.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the charioteer as the sole group with the authority to judge the technical correctness of chariot-driving descriptions, excluding others (like doctors or rhapsodes) from this specific immaterial resource.

verbatim: Is it for the doctor’s or the rhapsode’s art to discern aright whether Homer speaks correctly here or not? Ion. For the doctor’s.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the principle of artistic specialization, the doctor is identified as the group possessing the resource of expert judgment regarding medical descriptions in literature.

verbatim: be so good as to make out those also that are for the seer and the seer’s art, and show me the sort of passages that come under his ability to distinguish whether they are well or ill done; . . . This passage, and others of the sort, are those that I should say the seer has to examine and judge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly allocates the right to judge passages concerning omens and prophecy to the group of seers (mantis) based on their specific profession.

verbatim: My excellent Ion, you are acquainted with Apollodorus of Cyzicus, are you not? Ion. What might he be? Soc. A man whom the Athenians have often chosen as their general, though a foreigner; and Phanosthenes of Andros, and Heracleides of Clazomenae, whom my city invests with the high command and other offices although they are foreigners, because they have proved themselves to be competent.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Socrates lists specific historical examples (Apollodorus, Phanosthenes, and Heracleides) of non-Athenians being elected to the highest military office (strategos), demonstrating that during this period of Athenian history, foreign birth was not an absolute legal bar to holding high command if one was deemed competent.
